retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Chondrocytes -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Chondrocytes are mainly responsible for the production of collagen and the extracellular matrix that will lead to the maintenance of cartilaginous tissues within joints. Initial cartilage is composed of the mesenchyme during the fifth week of development. The mesenchyme activates in areas of chondral development and condenses to make chondrification centers. The mesenchymal cells develop into prechondrocytes, which later become chondroblasts; chondroblasts secrete collagenous fibrils and extracellular matrix. Consequently, collagenous and elastic fibers are stored within the intercellular matrix. The type of matrix composed contributes to cartilaginous typecast; there are mainly three types of cartilage: Hyaline cartilage: This type of cartilage is the most widespread and is usually present within joints. Fibrocartilage: This type of cartilage is part of the intervertebral disc. Elastic cartilage: This type of cartilage is in the Eustachian tubes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Joints -- Structure and Function -- Cartilaginous Joints
retrieved_docs.content: Cartilaginous joints have bones attached by hyaline or fibrous cartilage. The joints are further classified as primary or secondary cartilaginous joints, depending on the cartilage type involved.

retrieved_docs.title: Chondrosarcoma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Chondrosarcomas are malignant cartilaginous neoplasms with diverse morphological features and clinical behavior. They account for about 20% of all primary malignant tumors of the bone [1] . They usually arise in the pelvis or long bones [2] . Primary or conventional chondrosarcoma arises in preexisting normal bone and is distinguished from the rarer secondary tumors, which occur in a preexisting enchondroma or osteochondroma [1] . Conventional chondrosarcoma, which accounts for 85%–90% of chondrosarcomas is subdivided into the central, periosteal, and peripheral subgroups [3] . Non-conventional chondrosarcoma variants include clear cell chondrosarcoma, mesenchymal chondrosarcoma, and dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma [3] . The radiographic features of chondrosarcoma are often very characteristic, and a definitive diagnosis can usually be made by imaging examination alone.

retrieved_docs.title: Synovial Chondromatosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The initial diagnosis is made through a thorough history, physical examination, and radiological evaluation of an affected joint. These joints usually present as having a large effusion and sometimes appear deformed due to swelling or synovial hypertrophy. They are painful at rest and are more painful with motion. The range of motion is usually decreased. X-ray evaluation reveals multiple loose radio-opaque chondroid bodies of varied sizes in a joint. MRI arthrogram enables better evaluation of the process and the extent of involvement within the joint and surrounding tissue. Synovial chondromatosis is usually monoarticular, although rare cases of multiple joint involvements occur. The process may occur in two forms: primary synovial chondromatosis (also referred to as synovial osteochondromatosis, synovial chondrosis, Reichel syndrome, or synovial chondrometaplasia) and secondary synovial chondromatosis). [5] [6] The primary and secondary forms have different presentations and, as such, are treated differently. In rare cases, an extra-articular form of the condition can be identified. In these cases, the lesions are found in bursal tissue and/or in tenosynovial tissue in proximity to an involved joint. [7] [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Musculoskeletal Examination -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The musculoskeletal system (MSK) forms the structural components of the body; muscles, bones, joints, and connective tissues like tendons and ligaments surrounding these structures. The musculoskeletal examination is composed of several clinical tests. Broadly, a musculoskeletal system exam could classify as a: Screening MS exam- a quick assessment of overall structure and function Comprehensive MS exam - detailed exam is typically done by rheumatologists Regional/focused MS exam - more specific evaluation of particular joint or other structure

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Chondrocytes -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Endochondral ossification is crucial for skeletal development, specifically in preexisting cartilaginous models. The primary ossification center in long bones first appears in diaphysis (a portion of a long bone between two ends) that develops into the shaft of a bone. Chondrocytes undergo hypertrophy and enlarge at the sites of ossification. At these sites, the matrix becomes calcified and cellular necrosis appears. Osteochondral ossification is further regulated by the SOX9 transcription factor and the CARM1 (coactivator-associated arginine methyltransferase 1). Long bone elongation takes place at the diaphyseal-epiphyseal junction. Elongation directly correlates to cartilage plates made out of chondral matrix and collagen that proliferate and participate in endochondral osteogenesis and postnatal development. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Osteoarthritis -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: In a healthy synovial joint, bone ends are linked by an elastic layer of articular hyaline cartilage. This layer mainly consists of water, type II collagen that maintains the structure, and proteoglycans that provide the cartilage with the ability to withstand compressive and shear forces.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Joints -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A joint is a region where 2 bones make contact. Joints may be classified histologically or functionally. Histological classification is based on the predominant connective tissue type composing the joint, either fibrous, cartilaginous, or synovial. Functional classification is based on the amount of movement the joint permits. The 3 functional joint types include the immovable synarthrosis, slightly moveable amphiarthrosis, and freely moveable diarthrosis. The 2 joint classification schemes correlate: synarthroses are fibrous, amphiarthroses are cartilaginous, and diarthroses are synovial. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Larynx Arytenoid Cartilage -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx begins to form around the fourth week of development as an outpouching of the ventral wall of the primitive pharynx called laryngotracheal groove (or foregut). The development follows a craniocaudal progression, with the more superior thyroid cartilage arising from the fourth branchial arch, and the more caudal cricoid cartilage arising from the sixth branchial arch. The cartilaginous tissues from the fourth and sixth branchial arches merge and form the arytenoid, thyroid, cricoid, corniculate, and cuneiform cartilages, except the epiglottis which comes from the third arch. The fifth branchial arch may play a role in the origin of the arytenoid and corniculate cartilages, but due to its rapid degeneration during the human development, its function is still unclear. The laryngeal cartilages, including the epiglottis, originate from the mesenchymal tissue adjacent to both the entoderm and endoderm. In this tissue, small foci of cells show a trend towards chondroitic differentiation. The complete confluence of these foci and the formation of the definitive cartilages occur mostly postnatally. Arytenoid cartilages are of hyaline type (except the vocal processes) as well as thyroid and cricoid cartilages; the other cartilages consist of elastic cartilage. Hyaline cartilages tend to ossify after the age of 18 years, earlier in men than in women. In contrast to hyaline cartilages, ossification does not occur in elastic cartilages.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Cartilage -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cartilage has many functions, including the ability to resist compressive forces, enhance bone resilience, and provide support in bony areas where flexibility is needed (see Image. Cartilage and Bone Illustration). The primary cell that makes cartilage is the chondrocyte, which resides within the lacunae. The cartilage matrix consists of fibrous tissue and various combinations of proteoglycans and glycosaminoglycans. Cartilage, once synthesized, lacks lymphatic or blood supply, and the movement of waste and nutrition is chiefly done via diffusion to and from adjacent tissues. Cartilage, like bone, is surrounded by a perichondrium-like fibrous membrane. This layer is not efficient at regenerating cartilage. Hence, its recovery is slow after injury. The lack of active blood flow is the major reason any injury to cartilage takes a long time to heal. Cartilage has no nerve innervation; hence, there is no sensation when injured or damaged. When cartilage is calcified, the chondrocytes die. This is followed by the replacement of cartilage with bone-like tissue. Unlike bone, cartilage does not have calcium in the matrix. Instead, it contains high amounts of chondroitin, which is the material that provides elasticity and flexibility.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is classified as other non‑traumatic joint disorders and its primary finding site is the joints (klouby). Other non‑traumatic joint disorders are linked to the concept of اعتلال مفصلي (joint disease). In the context of surgical procedures, Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage is a subcategory (has parent) of Incision and excision of joint structures and it classifies the anatomical category “division of joint capsule; ligament or cartilage.” Incision and excision of joint structures also has a relationship with the joints (klouby). This integrated knowledge connects endocrine‑related arthropathy to non‑traumatic joint pathology, emphasizes its joint involvement, and outlines the hierarchical relationship between joint‑focused surgical interventions and the specific joint structures they address.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1866
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage” functions as a classification category that organizes the anatomical subdivisions referred to as “division of joint capsule; ligament or cartilage,” thereby grouping these related joint structures under a common descriptive heading.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 6 -- Community 2935
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an allowed qualifier defines which methods are considered permissible in clinical practice. Linked to the allowed qualifier are several specific methods: the Arabic‑named technique التمنيع, the surgical excision of semilunar cartilage of the knee, and the radiographic imaging of the joint referred to as صورة المفصل الشعاعية. This relationship shows that the allowed qualifier encompasses both therapeutic interventions, such as excision of semilunar cartilage, and diagnostic procedures, such as joint X‑ray imaging, as well as particular procedural terminology like التمنيع.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 469
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is classified under other non‑traumatic joint disorders. This arthropathy has its primary finding site in the joints (klouby) and is linked to the broader concept of joint disease (اعتلال مفصلي). Recognizing that this condition maps to other non‑traumatic joint disorders and involves the joints helps clinicians understand how endocrine or metabolic disturbances can produce non‑traumatic arthropathies that belong to the overall spectrum of joint disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1853

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1921
retrieved_docs.content: Arthrotomy with removal of prosthesis is a specific therapeutic intervention performed on joints. It is categorized under the broader group of other or therapeutic procedures on joints, indicating that it is one of several surgical options aimed at treating joint pathology. Additionally, Arthrotomy with removal of prosthesis is identified as a type of بضع المفصل, linking it to the general concept of joint removal procedures. This classification highlights its role within the spectrum of joint surgeries and underscores its relevance for clinicians selecting appropriate operative strategies for prosthetic complications or severe joint disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1854
retrieved_docs.content: The arthroscopic procedure of joint, not elsewhere classified is primarily mapped to the category of other operations on joint structures, indicating that it belongs to the broader group of surgical interventions targeting joint anatomy. In addition, this specific arthroscopic procedure is linked to the general concept of arthroscopy, from which it is derived, reflecting that it represents a particular application of arthroscopic techniques that is not captured elsewhere.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1721

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is classified as a form of other non‑traumatic joint disorders and its clinical findings are located in the joints (klouby). The category of other non‑traumatic joint disorders is linked to the Arabic term اعتلال مفصلي, which denotes joint disease. Arthrotomy with removal of prosthesis is a surgical intervention that is performed directly on the joints (klouby) and is considered an inverse isa of بضع المفصل, indicating that it represents a specific type of joint removal procedure. The operative concept of Division of joint capsule encompasses two subtypes—Division of joint capsule of hand and Division of joint capsule of wrist—each defined as an isa relationship to the broader Division of joint capsule. This broader Division of joint capsule also has a relationship to Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage, a category that classifies procedures involving the division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage. Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage is a child (has parent) of Incision and excision of joint structures, and the Incision and excision of joint structures category itself is related to the joints (klouby). Together, these relationships integrate disease classification, anatomical localization, and surgical techniques relevant to joint pathology and its management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2546
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure termed “Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage” is classified as a type of “Incision and excision of joint structures,” indicating that it falls under the broader category of surgical interventions that involve cutting and removing joint components. Additionally, this procedure specifically classifies the anatomical entities referred to as “division of joint capsule; ligament or cartilage,” delineating the particular joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage structures that are targeted during the incision and excision process.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 155
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is a pathological finding that may represent a wide spectrum of primary malignancies—including cancer of the kidney and renal pelvis, cancer of the ovary, pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي), bone cancer (سرطانة العظم), and malignant neoplasm without specification of site—and, when identified in the supraclavicular lymph nodes, is classified as Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes, a Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion located in the lymphatic tissue of the supraclavicular region. The presence of Tumor cells, malignant (or Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant) constitutes the abnormal cellular hallmark of these cancers, whereas Tumor cells, benign are explicitly excluded as disease findings for all listed malignancies. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1174
retrieved_docs.content: shared therapeutic goals for musculoskeletal and connective‑tissue conditions. This integrated framework supports comprehensive patient care by aligning specific modalities with appropriate clinical indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1047
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is classified as a form of other non‑traumatic joint disorders and its primary anatomic site is the joints (klouby). In the classification system, other non‑traumatic joint disorders are linked to the Arabic term اعتلال مفصلي, which also denotes joint disease. The anatomical concept of Division of joint capsule includes two specific subdivisions—Division of joint capsule of hand and Division of joint capsule of wrist—each identified as a type of Division of joint capsule. These subdivisions are further related to the broader category Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage, which serves to classify divisions of the joint capsule, ligaments, or cartilage. This broader category is a sub‑entity of the procedural concept Incision and excision of joint structures, indicating that surgical incision or excision may be performed on these joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage divisions. The procedure Incision and excision of joint structures is also associated with the joints (klouby). Collectively, these relationships illustrate how endocrine‑related arthropathy is grouped with non‑traumatic joint diseases, how joint capsule anatomy is organized into specific hand and wrist divisions, and how these anatomical divisions are connected to surgical interventions on the joints.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2851
retrieved_docs.content: Other non‑traumatic joint disorders are closely associated with joint disease (اعتلال مفصلي), indicating that a range of non‑injury‑related joint conditions fall under the broader category of joint disease. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing various non‑traumatic joint pathologies as part of the overall spectrum of اعتلال مفصلي for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1923
retrieved_docs.content: Incision and excision of joint structures is the overarching surgical category that encompasses the more specific procedure called Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage. The Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage is classified as a division of joint capsule; ligament or cartilage, indicating that it involves separating these particular joint components. Moreover, Incision and excision of joint structures is linked to klouby, the term for joints, underscoring that both the general and specific procedures pertain to joint anatomy. This hierarchical and classificatory framework clarifies how the targeted division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage fits within the broader context of joint structure incision and excision, supporting precise procedural planning and documentation in patient care.
